drift.summary: The AgentLoop control flow is aligned across the twins (identical maxSteps=6 + max(1,·) clamp, cancel-check at each step boundary, parseFailures>=2 → planError, stall>=2 → stalled, final-answer safety gate before the stall guard, per-tool and per-plan safety gates, plan→Capability resolution with the same unknown-tool message, identical record/prevSig/lastObs timing, and all 6 HaltReason cases + userMessage strings match; the run() param reorder is deliberate/documented and behavior-neutral). The DRIFT is entirely in AgentDecisionParser's plan path. Swift parses with JSONSerialization and casts object["plan"] as? [[String:Any]]; when "plan" is present but is NOT a clean array of objects, that cast fails and Swift FALLS THROUGH to the top-level "tool" key. Kotlin's hand-rolled extractArray matches on "plan":[…] regardless of element types and its ?.let block ALWAYS returns — it never falls through. This produces three verified divergences when a (local) model emits a plan array with non-object members. Empirically confirmed by compiling and running both parsers on identical inputs.

drifts.description: Plan array containing a non-object (garbage) member alongside a valid object diverges into two DIFFERENT executed actions. Swift's `object["plan"] as? [[String:Any]]` cast rejects the whole mixed array (a String element cannot bridge to [String:Any]) and falls through to the top-level `tool` key, so Swift runs useTool(top-level-tool). Kotlin's extractArray returns the array body regardless of element types, splitObjects only collects the `{...}` members (silently dropping the non-object garbage), and since calls.size == objects.size holds over just the surviving objects, Kotlin returns Plan([...]) and never reaches the tool fallback. Root cause: Swift's strict array-of-objects cast + fall-through vs Kotlin's presence-based extractArray + unconditional `return` in the `?.let` block.
drifts.failureScenario: Model emits {"plan":[{"tool":"a"},"garbage"],"tool":"fallback","input":"x"} (torn/mixed JSON, exactly the local-model failure mode these parsers were hardened against). Verified by execution: Swift returns useTool(name=fallback) and runs the fallback tool; Kotlin returns Plan([ToolCall(a,"")]) and runs a one-step plan. Same model output → two entirely different tool executions per platform.

confirm.reason:
CONFIRMED — real behavioral drift, not a false positive. Traced both cited parsers on the failure input `{"plan":[{"tool":"a"},"garbage"],"tool":"fallback","input":"x"}` (a plan array mixing one valid object with a non-object string — exactly the torn/mixed local-model output these parsers were hardened against).

Swift (AgentDecision.swift:39-51): `object["plan"] as? [[String: Any]]` is an all-or-nothing cast. JSONSerialization parses the mixed array as `[Any]` (one dict + one String); the String cannot bridge to `[String:Any]`, so the WHOLE cast returns nil, the plan branch is skipped, and control falls through to line 49 `object["tool"] as? String` → returns `.useTool(name:"fallback", input:"x")`. Swift's `compactMap` + `calls.count == planItems.count` check never even runs, because `planItems` only binds when the entire array is typed as dicts.

Kotlin (AgentDecision.kt:25-38): `extractArray` is a type-blind string brace/bracket-walker — it returns the raw array body `{"tool":"a"},"garbage"` regardless of element types (its inner capture only tracks `[`/`]` for bracketDepth and skips strings, so the nested `{...}` and the trailing garbage string don't disturb it). `splitObjects` then collects ONLY the `{...}` members, silently dropping `"garbage"`, yielding `objects=[{"tool":"a"}]` (size 1). `calls` = `[ToolCall("a","")]` (size 1). The guard `calls.isNotEmpty() && calls.size == objects.size` is `1 == 1` → true, so the `extractArray(...)?.let { ... }` block executes `return AgentDecision.Plan([ToolCall("a","")])` and NEVER reaches the `tool` fallback below it. The size-parity check runs over the surviving objects, so the dropped garbage element is invisible to Kotlin but fatal to Swift.

Same model output → Swift runs `useTool(fallback)` (single tool-use path), Kotlin runs `Plan([a])` (aggregate-plan path via runner.executePlan). In AgentLoop these are entirely different execution branches — different tool(s) invoked, different consent/approval UX, different ledgering — so the drift is materially observable, not inert. Aligned cases (empty plan, all-garbage plan) both return nil on both sides; the divergence is specifically the MIXED case (≥1 valid object + ≥1 non-object member). Root cause exactly as the first reviewer stated: Swift's strict array-of-objects cast + fall-through vs Kotlin's presence-based extractArray + unconditional return in the `?.let` block. This is surrounding-logic drift, not a decision-vector the parity checker already guards.

drifts.description: Plan value that is an array of non-object primitives (strings or numbers) present alongside a top-level `tool` key. Swift's `as? [[String:Any]]` cast fails on the primitive elements and falls through to the `tool` key, returning useTool(top-level-tool). Kotlin's extractArray matches "plan":[…], splitObjects finds zero `{...}` objects → objects=[] → calls=[] → the `?.let` returns null (calls.isEmpty). So Kotlin yields no decision (parser returns null → loop nudges as a parse failure, eventually PLAN_ERROR), while Swift executes a tool. Different halt path AND different side effects.
drifts.failureScenario: Model emits {"plan":["a","b"],"tool":"calc","input":"2+2"} (or {"plan":[1,2],...}). Verified by execution: Swift returns useTool(name=calc) and runs the calculator; Kotlin returns null, so the AgentLoop counts a parseFailure (nudge + retry, PLAN_ERROR on the second miss) and runs no tool. Identical input, divergent outcome per platform.

confirm.reason:
CONFIRMED — real behavioral drift. Traced the reviewer's exact input `{"plan":["a","b"],"tool":"calc","input":"2+2"}` through both parsers.

SWIFT (AgentDecision.swift:39-51): `object["plan"] as? [[String: Any]]` (line 39) — JSONSerialization deserializes `["a","b"]` as NSArray of NSString; the conditional cast to `[[String:Any]]` is all-or-nothing and nils out because the elements are strings, not dictionaries. The whole plan `if let` is skipped. Control falls through to line 49 `object["tool"] as? String` = "calc" (nonempty) → returns `.useTool(name:"calc", input:"2+2")`. The AgentLoop (AgentLoop.swift:88, 127-134) then safety-gates and EXECUTES the calculator.

KOTLIN (AgentDecision.kt:25-38): `extractArray(json,"plan")` (line 25) only checks the value is a balanced `[...]` (extractArray lines 69-86) — contents are irrelevant, so it returns the non-null body `"a","b"`. That makes the `?.let` (line 25) ENTER, and its lambda ALWAYS hits a `return` (line 33), so `parse` returns from inside the `.let` and NEVER falls through to the `tool` key at line 35. `splitObjects` (line 28) finds zero `{` → objects=[] → calls=[] → line 33 `calls.isNotEmpty()` is false → returns **null**. The AgentLoop (AgentLoop.kt:72-79) counts a parseFailure, nudges, and on the second identical miss halts with PLAN_ERROR — NO tool runs.

The asymmetry is structural, not a misread: Swift validates plan-element SHAPE at the cast (line 39) and falls through to `tool` on failure; Kotlin validates shape LATER inside the already-entered plan branch (splitObjects) and can no longer reach `tool`. Same input → useTool(calc)+execution on iOS/macOS vs null→PLAN_ERROR+no execution on Android. Different halt reason AND different side effects. Verified the `answer` key is absent on both (Swift:36 / Kotlin:24 both false), so both genuinely reach the plan branch. This is distinct from the decision-vector parity check — it is drift in the plan-vs-tool precedence/fall-through on a malformed-plan input. Both cited line ranges are accurate.

drift.summary: Line-by-line comparison of all three twin pairs. AgentSession.swift/kt are behaviorally aligned: the cancel()/requestCancel() flag semantics, the per-run fresh flag (CancelFlag() vs cancelRequested=false), the reentrancy guard (guard !isRunning vs synchronized isRunning), the isRunning-in-defer/finally reset, and the final answer/haltReason/steps fields all match. The onStep live-streaming (Kotlin) vs final-transcript-only (Swift) difference is documented, intentional, and produces the same final `steps`, so it is not a drift. The AgentTool ArithmeticParser is aligned on precedence, right-assoc power, unary-minus binding, the maxDepth=100 recursion cap, /-and-%-by-zero guards, truncatingRemainder-vs-%= (both truncated), pow, pi/e constants, the function table, NaN/Inf rejection, and every token edge case (bare '.', '1.2.3', '1e5', invalid operator char). The integer-render guards (rounded()==v vs ==rint(v)) are equivalent because a true integer has no tie to break. Four real drifts remain, all in the two peripheral tools plus the shared tokenizer/stringify seams: unit-format rounding mode, date-offset overflow crash, isNumber-vs-isDigit Unicode tokenizing, and Double->String rendering.

drifts.description: UnitConverter.format uses a different rounding mode when rendering a non-integer conversion result to 4 decimals. Swift uses (v*10000).rounded()/10000 where Double.rounded() rounds half AWAY from zero; Kotlin uses Math.round(v*10000)/10000.0 where Math.round is floor(x+0.5), i.e. half UP (toward +infinity). The two agree for positive values but diverge for negative values landing exactly on a 5th-decimal half-boundary.
drifts.failureScenario: A conversion whose result is a negative value like -0.00025 (i.e. v*10000 = -2.5 exactly). Swift .rounded() gives -3 -> formats as "-0.0003"; Kotlin Math.round gives -2 -> formats as "-0.0002". Same input yields a different displayed magnitude per platform.

confirm.reason:
CONFIRMED — this is a real behavioral drift, not a false positive. Both cited lines match exactly: Swift AgentToolsExtra.swift:111 `String((v * 10000).rounded() / 10000)` and Kotlin AgentToolsExtra.kt:113 `(Math.round(v * 10000) / 10000.0).toString()`. Swift's argument-less `Double.rounded()` rounds half AWAY from zero; Java/Kotlin `Math.round(double)` is defined as `floor(a + 0.5)`, i.e. half toward +infinity. They agree for positive values but diverge for negatives landing exactly on a 5th-decimal half boundary. I verified in IEEE-754 double that the reviewer's example `-0.00025 * 10000` is EXACTLY -2.5 (not an approximation), so Swift yields -3 → "-0.0003" while Kotlin yields -2 → "-0.0002" — a different displayed value per platform. Reproduced for -0.00025, -0.00035, -0.00045 as well.

Reachability confirmed (the key skeptic check): format() is called on req.value (parsed user input) at Swift:23 / Kotlin:23, and the number parser explicitly accepts a leading '-' (Swift line 88, Kotlin line 85), so a user CAN enter a negative value. It is also called on `result`, which can be negative for sub-zero temperature conversions. The integer short-circuit guard on the line above (v.rounded()==v / v==Math.rint(v)) does NOT catch these values — -0.00025 is non-integer, so both sides fall through to the divergent rounding line. This is user-visible display formatting, not an agent decision vector, so it is not covered by the agent-parity checker and is in scope. The reviewer correctly identified both locations, the rounding-mode semantics, and the negative-only divergence; wrongSide "both-disagree" and severity P2 (rare-input cosmetic magnitude difference) are appropriate. Not refuted.

drifts.description: DateCalc offset ('plus/minus N days') differs on out-of-range results. Swift Calendar.date(byAdding:.day,value:to:) returns an Optional Date; an out-of-range result yields nil which the guard converts to the graceful 'Couldn't read a date calculation' fallback. Kotlin dates[0].plusDays(n.toLong()) THROWS java.time.DateTimeException on year overflow and nothing catches it in DateCalc.evaluate / DateCalcTool.run, so it propagates as an uncaught exception.
drifts.failureScenario: Input like '2026-06-08 plus 2000000000 days' (firstInteger reads a near-Int.MAX offset, ~5.8M years, exceeding LocalDate max year 999999999). Swift returns the friendly 'Couldn't read...' message; Kotlin throws DateTimeException and crashes the tool call instead of returning a message, violating the file's stated 'tolerate LLM garbage, never throw' contract.

confirm.reason:
CONFIRMED — real behavioral drift, wrong side is Kotlin, exactly as the first reviewer claims. Both cited lines are accurate: Swift apple/QuenderinKit/Sources/QuenderinKit/AgentToolsExtra.swift:196 `guard let result = utcCalendar().date(byAdding: .day, value: isMinus ? -n : n, to: dates[0]) else { return nil }`; Kotlin android/quenderin-core/src/main/kotlin/ai/quenderin/core/AgentToolsExtra.kt:165 `return dates[0].plusDays((if (isMinus) -n else n).toLong()).toString()`.

The divergence: (1) Swift `Calendar.date(byAdding:)` returns `Date?` and NEVER throws — `Date` is backed by a Double TimeInterval whose representable range (~±10^290 yr) dwarfs any Int-sized day offset, so a ~2-billion-day offset yields a valid (absurd) Date, formatted into a string. No crash. (2) Kotlin `LocalDate.plusDays(n)` THROWS `java.time.DateTimeException` on year overflow (LocalDate caps at year ±999,999,999; ~2e9 days ≈ 5.8M yr blows past it). The only `runCatching` in the Kotlin file is inside `isoDates` (line 134), guarding date PARSING, not the offset addition. Nothing in `firstInteger`/`DateCalc.evaluate`/`DateCalcTool.run` catches it, and neither AgentSession (Swift or Kotlin) nor the tool boundary wraps `.run()` in a catch — so the exception propagates uncaught, violating the file-header contract shared by both twins ("Every entry point tolerates LLM-generated garbage and returns a message rather than throwing").

Trigger window is real and reachable: `firstInteger` returns `Int?`. Kotlin Int is 32-bit, so offsets from ~3.65M days (10k yr, overflows LocalDate) up to Int.MAX (2.147e9) fit the Int parse yet overflow LocalDate — a wide, easily-hit range; the reviewer's ~2e9-day example sits squarely inside it. Above Int.MAX both sides gracefully return null (toIntOrNull → null), so that's not the drift; the drift is this mid-range band.

One minor imprecision in the reviewer's writeup, which does NOT refute the drift: for a ~2e9-day input Swift does NOT return the friendly 'Couldn't read...' fallback — it returns a garbage-but-valid date string (the nil path is essentially never taken for Int-sized offsets). Either way the core claim holds and is if anything worse: on identical input, Swift degrades without throwing while Kotlin throws an uncaught DateTimeException and crashes the tool call. This is a genuine per-platform bug, not a parity-checker-guarded decision vector.

drifts.description: The calculator tokenizer classifies numeric characters differently. Swift uses Character.isNumber (true for ANY Unicode numeric: superscripts like U+00B2 '2', vulgar fractions like U+00BD '1/2', Roman numerals, circled digits), while Kotlin uses Char.isDigit() (true only for Unicode category Nd decimal digits). The same divergence appears in UnitConverter.parse's number-prefix scan (Swift AgentToolsExtra.swift:88 isNumber vs Kotlin AgentToolsExtra.kt:85 isDigit()).
drifts.failureScenario: Adversarial/LLM input containing a Unicode numeric non-digit, e.g. '2 to 5' where the second char is superscript-two U+00B2, or '5² + 1'. Swift's isNumber appends it to the number buffer (then Double() fails on that buffer -> nil), whereas Kotlin's isDigit() rejects it as an operator char and returns null/empty tokens. Both ultimately reject, but the tokenization path and any partial parse differ; for the unit-parse number scan the from-unit boundary is computed at a different index across platforms.

confirm.reason:
CONFIRMED as a real (P3) drift. Both cited locations are accurate: Swift uses Character.isNumber at AgentTool.swift:66 and AgentToolsExtra.swift:88, while Kotlin uses Char.isDigit() at AgentTool.kt:65 and AgentToolsExtra.kt:85. The technical claim is correct — Swift's isNumber matches ANY Unicode numeric (superscripts U+00B2, vulgar fractions U+00BD, Roman numerals, circled digits) whereas Kotlin's isDigit() only matches category-Nd decimal digits, so a Unicode-numeric non-digit is classified differently.

I traced both paths concretely.

CALCULATOR path (e.g. "5² + 1"): the divergence is INERT. Swift appends ² to the number buffer, producing token "5²"; Double("5²") returns nil (Swift's strict C-locale Double init rejects superscripts) → "Couldn't evaluate". Kotlin routes ² to the else branch, ² is not in "+-*/^%()" → tokenize returns null → "Couldn't evaluate". Both platforms emit the IDENTICAL rejection string, so the calculator half is a false-alarm on its own — the reviewer correctly noted "both ultimately reject."

UNIT-PARSE path (e.g. "5² m to ft") is where the drift becomes OBSERVABLE. On left="5² m": Swift's scan consumes both 5 and ² into numStr="5²", Double("5²")=nil → parse returns nil → output "Couldn't read a conversion from \"5² m to ft\". Try \"20 km to mi\"." Kotlin's scan stops at ² (numStr="5"), "5".toDoubleOrNull()=5.0, then fromUnit=canonical("² m")="² m" (non-empty), yielding Request(5.0,"² m","ft"); convert() finds "² m" absent from the linear map → output "Can't convert ² m to ft — they measure different things." Two DIFFERENT strings returned to the agent for the same input — a genuine per-platform behavioral divergence, exactly the "from-unit boundary computed at a different index" the finding describes.

Caveats supporting the P3 severity: (1) neither platform ever produces a WRONG numeric result — both only reach different rejection MESSAGES; (2) it requires adversarial Unicode-numeric input in the numeric prefix specifically; (3) the calculator half of the finding is inert. But the unit-parse divergence is real and observable, so the finding is not a false positive. wrongSide "both-disagree" is accurate — Swift's isNumber and Kotlin's isDigit() are simply inconsistent; the fix is to align both (Kotlin's isDigit() is the stricter, safer choice). I read all six named files in full and found no additional undisputed drift affecting this specific finding.

drifts.description: Non-integer numeric results are stringified with the platform's native Double formatter, which differ. Swift String(value) uses Swift's Double.description; Kotlin value.toString() uses Java Double.toString. They diverge on scientific-notation thresholds and formatting (e.g. Swift '1e+20' vs Kotlin '1.0E20'; Swift '1e-07' vs Kotlin '1.0E-7'; Swift '123456789012345.6' vs Kotlin '1.234567890123456E14'). Same divergence in UnitConverter.format's fallback (Swift AgentToolsExtra.swift:111 String(...) vs Kotlin AgentToolsExtra.kt:113 .toString()).
drifts.failureScenario: calculator input '1/3' or '2^70': the numeric value is identical on both platforms but the observation string the planner/user sees differs (e.g. Swift '1e+21' vs Kotlin '1.0E21'), producing a different tool observation feeding back into the agent loop across platforms.

confirm.reason:
CONFIRMED real drift, not a false positive. Empirically verified with the actual toolchains (Swift 6.2.4 + OpenJDK 17): Swift String(Double) and Java/Kotlin Double.toString() use different shortest-representation algorithms and diverge on scientific-notation formatting.

Both cited locations are reachable with identical numeric value but divergent observation string, because both twins share the SAME guard, so the same inputs fall through to the divergent branch on both platforms:

LOCATION 1 — CalculatorTool (AgentTool.swift:44 `return String(value)` vs AgentTool.kt:40 `... else value.toString()`; reviewer cited kt:41, trivially off by one, logic unambiguous). Shared guard `abs(value) < 1e15` gates the integer fast-path on both sides, so non-integer or >=1e15 results hit the divergent branch identically. Confirmed:
- 2^70 (reviewer's example): value 1.1805916207174113e21 identical on both (both use IEEE pow, isFinite so not rejected) → iOS observation "1.1805916207174113e+21" vs Android "1.1805916207174113E21".
- non-integer like 123456789012345.6 → iOS "123456789012345.6" vs Android "1.234567890123456E14".
- 1e15 → iOS "1000000000000000.0" vs Android "1.0E15".

LOCATION 2 — UnitConverter.format fallback (AgentToolsExtra.swift:111 `String((v*10000).rounded()/10000)` vs AgentToolsExtra.kt:113 `(Math.round(v*10000)/10000.0).toString()`). Shared guard `abs(v) < 1e12`. 4-dp rounding does NOT bound magnitude, so large conversions diverge. Confirmed a REAL reachable query: "100000 week to ms" = 100000*604800/0.001 = identical value on both, but iOS formats "60480000000000.0" vs Android "6.048E13". Input-side format(req.value) with a >=1e12 input value diverges too (5e12 → "5000000000000.0" vs "5.0E12").

Classification "both-disagree" is correct: the value is identical, only the stringified tool observation fed back into the agent loop differs per platform. The parity checker guards decision vectors, not this surrounding string-formatting logic, so it is not covered. This is a genuine per-platform behavioral drift.

Ran side-by-side (excerpt): 1e-7 → Swift "1e-07" / Java "1.0E-7"; 1e20 → "1e+20" / "1.0E20"; 2^60 even differs in mantissa digits ("1.152921504606847e+18" / "1.15292150460684698E18"). Ordinary mid-magnitude decimals (1/3, 0.1, 2.5, 1/7) agree, so the drift is confined to scientific-notation/large-magnitude inputs — but those ARE reachable via the calculator (2^70) and units (large conversions).

drift.summary: Line-by-line comparison of AgentRunExporter.swift vs AgentRunExporter.kt found no behavioral drift. Both render identical Markdown: same heading/trim + "Agent run" fallback, same step-count pluralization (n==1 ? "" : "s"), and all 6 halt-reason status strings match exactly (answered / stopped at the step limit / stopped by the safety filter / stopped (could not form a plan) / stopped (stuck repeating a step) / stopped (you halted it)). Tools-used collection dedupes and preserves first-seen order on both sides — Swift uses an array with `!contains` guards for both .useTool and .plan cases, Kotlin uses a LinkedHashSet with .add for both; net result is identical. Step rendering (Used `name`(input) / Proposed a plan: joined `name`(input) / Final answer), the ` → observation` append gated on non-empty (Swift !isEmpty, Kotlin isNotEmpty), the answer-vs-halt tail (**Answer:** when answer present, else **Halted:** from userMessage which returns nil/null for ANSWERED), and the final trim+"\n" all match. The only cross-language nuance is Swift's `.whitespacesAndNewlines` trim vs Kotlin's `trim()` (Char.isWhitespace) — both strip leading/trailing whitespace and newlines and behave identically over the realistic goal/heading input domain; not a drift.

Line-by-line diff of ChatModel.swift vs ChatModel.kt. The isFlagged rule, engineText composition, windowing-before-placeholder, the 32-token degeneration cadence, the empty-reply notice string, the stillActive/!stopRequested settle guard, and reset/restore-cancel-before-mutate ordering all match. But three real behavioral drifts remain: (A) the empty-input precondition throws on Android vs silent no-op on iOS; (B) the degeneration guard checks the accumulated text one token earlier on Swift (check-then-append) than on Kotlin (append-then-check), and the abort action differs (Swift breaks the loop, Kotlin only requestCancel()s and keeps consuming buffered pieces), so the two abort at different points and retain different partial text; (C) the final settle uses different sources of truth — Swift settles the streamed accumulation (assistant.text), Kotlin settles the engine's return value (reply), which diverge on any non-streaming / post-cancel path.

drifts.description: Empty-input handling diverges. Swift's send() guards with `guard !trimmed.isEmpty || !documents.isEmpty, !isGenerating else { return }` — an empty message (no text, no documents) is a SILENT no-op. Kotlin's send() uses `require(trimmed.isNotEmpty() || documents.isNotEmpty()) { "Message is empty" }`, which THROWS IllegalArgumentException on the same empty input. Same user action produces opposite control flow: quietly ignored on iOS, an exception on Android.
drifts.failureScenario: User taps Send with an empty composer and no attachments (e.g. an accidental double-tap, or a UI that doesn't pre-validate). iOS ignores it and nothing happens. Android throws IllegalArgumentException("Message is empty") out of send(); if the Compose caller doesn't catch it (it has no reason to, since the iOS twin never throws), the send coroutine crashes / surfaces an error toast. Cross-platform UI code written against the iOS no-op contract breaks on Android.

confirm.reason:
CONFIRMED real drift — not a false positive. Both cited lines were read correctly. Swift ChatModel.swift:86 `guard !trimmed.isEmpty || !documents.isEmpty, !isGenerating else { return }` makes an empty send (no text, no documents) a SILENT no-op. Kotlin ChatModel.kt:88 `require(trimmed.isNotEmpty() || documents.isNotEmpty()) { "Message is empty" }` THROWS IllegalArgumentException on the identical input. Same user action → opposite control flow.

The divergence is neither inert nor intentional: both twins' own doc comments state the function is meant to HANDLE the empty case ("No-ops on empty input" on Swift line 81-83; "Documents alone are a legitimate send" on Kotlin line 87) — so both intend the same contract, and the Kotlin `require` is the outlier that turns a documented no-op into a crash. There is no compensating pre-validation in either file guaranteeing send is never called empty.

Notably the OTHER guard condition IS aligned: the already-generating case is a silent no-op on both sides (Swift line 86 `!isGenerating else { return }`; Kotlin line 101 `if (isGenerating) return ""`). Kotlin thus uses silent-return for the double-send guard but throw for the empty-input guard — inconsistent even with its own conventions, which reinforces that the empty-input throw is the accidental drift.

Failure scenario holds: an empty/accidental Send is ignored on iOS but crashes the send coroutine / surfaces an error on Android when the Compose caller doesn't catch (it has no reason to, since the iOS twin never throws). Kotlin is the side that breaks the documented no-op contract; `wrongSide: both-disagree` is acceptable. Fix: make Kotlin return early ("" ) on empty input to match Swift's silent no-op.

drifts.description: Degeneration-guard order-of-operations + abort-action drift. Swift increments tokenCount, checks looksDegenerate(assistant.text) on the text as it stands BEFORE the current token is appended, then does `assistant.text += token`, and on a hit `break`s the loop immediately (dropping the current token). Kotlin does `sb.append(piece)` FIRST, then increments and checks looksDegenerate(sb.toString()) — so at every %32 checkpoint the checked text is exactly one token LONGER than Swift's, and on a hit it calls `engine.requestCancel()` WITHOUT breaking, so the callback keeps firing for any already-buffered pieces until the engine actually terminates. Same token stream → guard evaluated against different text (off-by-one) and generation retained up to a different point.
drifts.failureScenario: A model whose repetition first becomes detectable exactly at a 32-token boundary. On iOS the guard fires having appended N-1 tokens and breaks, so the transcript ends one token before Android, which appended the Nth token then fired and lets a few more buffered pieces land before the native decode stops. Result: the two platforms retain visibly different partial replies for the identical degenerate stream (different truncation point, different amount of the looping tail), and the subsequent collapseRepeatedParagraphs runs over different inputs.

confirm.reason:
CONFIRMED real drift; not refuted. Both cited locations are accurate on inspection. Swift ChatModel.swift:118-120 increments tokenCount, checks DegenerationGuard.looksDegenerate(assistant.text) BEFORE appending the current token (append is line 120), and on a hit `break`s — so token 32 is never appended and the loop halts synchronously; retained text = tokens 1..31. Kotlin ChatModel.kt:131-138 does sb.append(piece) FIRST (line 127), then increments and checks looksDegenerate(sb.toString()) with token 32 already included, and on a hit calls engine.requestCancel() WITHOUT breaking (line 133) — the callback returns normally, so the current token is kept and any already-buffered pieces keep invoking the callback until the async cancel is honored; retained text = tokens 1..32 plus a buffered tail.

Two genuine, independent differences, both-disagree:
1. Off-by-one on the checked text: at every %32 checkpoint Kotlin feeds looksDegenerate exactly one more token than Swift (check-before-append vs append-before-check). Same stream, different input to the guard.
2. Halt mechanism / retained text: Swift drops the current token and stops immediately via iterator break; Kotlin keeps the current token and relies on requestCancel(), which the file's own contract (Swift 67-70, Kotlin 179-180) says lands late / cancels the native decode asynchronously, letting buffered pieces land.

Skeptical checks did not neutralize it: the %32 cadence, the threshold, and the settle-time collapseRepeatedParagraphs are all aligned, but collapse only removes exact-duplicate paragraph runs and cannot be relied on to erase a one-token / few-token tail difference — and it runs over different inputs on the two platforms. Kotlin cannot `break` because it is a callback into engine.completeChat (its only lever is requestCancel), which explains WHY the drift exists but does not make it inert. For a model whose repetition first becomes detectable exactly at a 32-token boundary, the two platforms retain visibly different partial replies (different truncation point, different amount of the looping tail). Real per-platform behavioral drift.

drifts.description: Settle source-of-truth drift. Swift's settle collapses the STREAMED accumulation: `assistant.text = DegenerationGuard.collapseRepeatedParagraphs(assistant.text)` — it has no other source, generation is purely stream-driven. Kotlin instead settles the ENGINE'S RETURN VALUE: `val settled = DegenerationGuard.collapseRepeatedParagraphs(reply)`, where `reply` is completeChat()'s return, explicitly to cover a non-streaming fallback where the onToken callback never fired (comment lines 140-141). When streamed text (sb) and engine return (reply) diverge — non-streaming engine path, or extra tokens the engine decoded after a degeneration/Stop requestCancel() but still included in its return — the two platforms settle different final text.

Android displays the full engine reply; iOS, having only the (empty or truncated) streamed assistant.text, either shows the empty-reply notice or a shorter reply for the identical generation. Also on a degeneration abort where the engine's return includes post-cancel tokens, Android's settled text is longer than iOS's break-truncated text.

drift.summary: The pure prompt-assembly path is byte-for-byte aligned across the twins: identical default system prompt, identical (contextTokens=4096, reservedForResponse=512) defaults, identical estimateTokens = max(1,(len+3)/4), identical "User:/Assistant:" line formatting (both emit exactly one space after the colon), identical windowedHistory loop (newest→oldest, keep-if-empty-or-fits, break, reverse), identical build() empty/trailing-newline/primer handling, and identical historyBudget overhead math. ContextWindow's three recommend() overloads match on every band edge (RAM 4/6, headroom 0.25/0.6/1.2), the *1.15 weights overhead, the /256 rounding, and the [256,8192] clamp — and Swift .rounded() vs Kotlin Math.round agree for all reachable (positive) inputs. On a memory-tight device whose loaded n_ctx is 512–2048, iOS will keep more history than the native window holds and overflow it — the exact bug Q-167 fixed on Android, still unfixed on iOS.

The Swift twin has no override parameter — windowedHistory and its historyBudget always use the configured contextTokens (default 4096), so it cannot be told the real native window size. This is a genuine per-platform behavioral divergence, not just an unused API: on iOS/macOS the history is always budgeted against 4096.
drifts.failureScenario: A model loads on iOS with a real n_ctx of 1024 (memory-tight device). Android's engine passes contextTokensOverride=1024, so windowedHistory trims history to fit ~1024 tokens and the prompt fits the native window. iOS has no way to pass 1024 — it budgets against contextTokens=4096, keeps ~4x more history, and the assembled prompt overflows the actual 1024-token native window, causing truncation/garbled output or a native context-overflow error on iOS/macOS that Android does not hit. drift.summary: Line-by-line comparison of all five twin pairs found real behavioral drift, not just cosmetic differences. The sharpest, fully-confirmed bug is a Unicode-whitespace collapse divergence in ConversationLibrary: Swift collapses ALL Unicode whitespace (Character.isWhitespace) while Kotlin's Regex(\"\\\\s+\") matches only ASCII whitespace, so a title/preview containing a no-break space (U+00A0), ideographic space (U+3000), etc. yields a different string and possibly a different truncation point on the two platforms — directly breaking the cross-platform title parity both files' comments explicitly promise (verified empirically). Second, the coordinator's persist() has opposite semantics mid-stream: Swift guards !isGenerating and skips saving without stopping, while Kotlin unconditionally stopGenerating() then saves the partial turn. Third, ConversationStore corruption recovery is per-row on Kotlin (mapNotNull salvages good lines) but all-or-nothing on Swift (a single JSONDecoder throw empties the whole transcript). Plus feature asymmetries: modelID (avatar/model tracking) and newChatSignal are wired through the entire Swift stack but absent from Kotlin, and deleteMany exists only on Swift. The pure decision-vector logic guarded by the parity checker was excluded. The differing wire formats (Swift JSON vs Kotlin escaped-TSV) are intentional and round-trip-equivalent, so not flagged.

drifts.description: Internal whitespace collapse diverges. Swift collapses runs with split(whereSeparator: { $0.isWhitespace }) (preview L70, title L84), where Character.isWhitespace matches ALL Unicode whitespace (U+00A0 no-break space, U+3000 ideographic space, U+2002 en space, U+2028 line separator, etc.). Kotlin collapses with split(Regex("\\s+")) (preview L62, title L79); Java's default \s (no UNICODE_CHARACTER_CLASS flag) matches ONLY ASCII [ \t\n\x0B\f\r] and does NOT match those Unicode spaces. Verified empirically. So the same input string produces a different collapsed title/preview on the two platforms — Swift replaces the exotic space with a normal space, Kotlin leaves it in. Because the code-point count at the 40/80 cap is then also different, truncation can land at a different boundary too. This directly breaks the cross-platform title parity the comments at both sites explicitly claim to guarantee.
drifts.failureScenario: User's first message is "Café menu ideas" (a no-break space between words, common from copy-paste or some keyboards). iOS history row title = "Café menu ideas" (normal space). Android title = "Café menu ideas" (retains U+00A0). The two devices show visibly/behaviorally different titles and previews for the identical conversation; near the 40-char cap they can even truncate at different points. Kotlin is wrong relative to the stated parity contract.

I read both ConversationLibrary twins in full and empirically ran the actual twin logic on both platforms (swiftc + kotlinc/openjdk available locally). Line citations are accurate on both sides. Empirical proof with the input "Café menu ideas" (U+00A0 no-break space): Swift collapsed the U+00A0 to a normal U+0020 space; Kotlin RETAINED the U+00A0. Same input, different persisted/displayed title on the two platforms. Second case: input with an ideographic space U+3000 → Swift collapses it, Kotlin retains it. The earlier trim step does NOT neutralize it (these are INTERNAL separators). Both sites carry comments explicitly promising cross-platform title/preview parity, so this violates a stated contract. Downstream: the collapsed code-point count differs, so truncation at the 40/80 cap can also land at a different boundary. Fix: give Kotlin the same Unicode reach (Pattern.UNICODE_CHARACTER_CLASS / `[\\s\\p{Z}]+`, or collapse via Char.isWhitespace to mirror Swift).

drifts.description: persist() has opposite behavior toward an in-flight reply. Swift guards `!chat.isGenerating` and early-returns WITHOUT saving or stopping — it declines to persist while a turn is streaming and leaves generation running. Kotlin instead calls chat.stopGenerating() UNCONDITIONALLY at the top and has NO isGenerating guard, so it kills the in-flight reply and then persists whatever partial transcript exists (provided count grew past savedCount). This is a genuine coordinator state-machine divergence on the exact mid-stream path both comment on (switch/open/new during generation).

On iOS: persist() sees isGenerating==true, returns immediately, the current chat is NOT saved and generation keeps running (open() then restores over it). On Android: persist() stops the stream and saves the half-streamed transcript. Same user action → iOS keeps generating and skips the save, Android halts and saves a partial turn. The two platforms leave different persisted state and different generation state.

drifts.description: Corruption-salvage guard is all-or-nothing on Swift but per-row on Kotlin. Kotlin decode() splits by line and mapNotNull-drops only the individually broken rows, keeping every salvageable message (L25-36). Swift decode() runs a single JSONDecoder().decode([StoredMessage].self) (L24): any structural JSON damage anywhere in the blob throws, and FileConversationPersistence.loadTranscript wraps it in `(try? store.decode(data)) ?? []`, so the WHOLE transcript becomes empty. Swift's compactMap only salvages the specific 'unparseable role' case within otherwise-valid JSON; it cannot salvage a torn/truncated blob the way Kotlin's line-based reader does.

On Android, loadTranscript keeps all the earlier intact messages and drops only the torn tail. On iOS, JSONDecoder throws and loadTranscript returns [] — the entire conversation appears empty/lost. Same corruption, whole conversation lost on iOS, mostly recovered on Android.

confirm.reason: CONFIRMED — real behavioral drift, both cited locations read correctly. Swift ConversationStore.decode() does a single JSONDecoder().decode([StoredMessage].self); any structural JSON damage anywhere in the blob throws, and FileConversationPersistence.loadTranscript wraps it `(try? store.decode(data)) ?? []`, so the ENTIRE transcript collapses to []. Kotlin decode() is line-delimited: split("\n").mapNotNull decodes each ROLE\ttext row independently, dropping only the individually broken lines and keeping every intact earlier row. So for the SAME logical corruption event — a transcript file whose tail is damaged/truncated — iOS returns [] (whole conversation lost) while Android returns the intact prefix (partial recovery). Atomic writes exist on both sides and lower frequency but do not equalize salvage behavior (Kotlin's own fallback path is non-atomic, and FS/media corruption is uncovered on both). "Wrong side = swift" is defensible: Kotlin's graceful per-row degradation is the intended behavior; Swift has no equivalent. Fix is not a one-line guard — Swift's JSON format structurally cannot do line-based salvage without a format change — so P2 is arguably slightly high, but the drift is real.

Kotlin has no bulk-delete entry point, so Android must delete conversations one at a time via delete(id), each of which independently runs the current-chat fallback (restore/open) if it hit the current chat. Deleting a selection that includes the current chat therefore cannot behave identically — Swift restores exactly once at the end, whereas any Android emulation via repeated delete() would restore intermediate conversations.

drift.summary:
Line-by-line comparison of the single `markdown(messages, title)` function in both twins shows identical behavior across every branch. Heading fallback: Swift `(trimmed?.isEmpty == false) ? trimmed! : "Conversation"` (nil title and whitespace-only title both fall through to "Conversation") matches Kotlin `title?.trim().takeUnless { it.isNullOrEmpty() } ?: "Conversation"`. Header line, message count, and pluralization (`count == 1 ? "" : "s"`) are identical. The message loop uses the same speaker mapping (`.user`/`Role.USER` -> "You" else "Quenderin") and the same per-message format `**<speaker>:**
<text>

`. Both finish with a whitespace trim of the full document plus a trailing "

The only micro-difference is the whitespace definition used for trimming (Swift `.whitespacesAndNewlines` vs Kotlin `trim()`/`Char.isWhitespace()`), which is applied identically on both sides and yields the same observable result for all normal inputs — not a bound/threshold/off-by-one drift. No behavioral drift found.

The two twins are structurally identical method-for-method (same window=160/threshold=3 defaults, same tailSpan=window*(threshold+2), same overlapping-match loop with end=hay.length-window and i<=end, same minLength=40 paragraph-collapse, same literal "\n\n" split). A secondary lower-confidence drift is trim() vs .whitespacesAndNewlines using not-provably-identical whitespace sets.

drifts.description: looksDegenerate measures length in different units. Swift builds `Array(text.unicodeScalars)` and uses `scalars.count`, `scalars.suffix(window)` etc. — one element per Unicode scalar. Kotlin uses `text.length`, `text.takeLast(window)`, `regionMatches` — all in UTF-16 code units, i.e. two units for every non-BMP (astral) character. So `window*threshold` short-circuit guard trips at a different input size, the `window`-char needle/hay slices span a different amount of real text, and `regionMatches`/scalar-array comparison align on different boundaries whenever the text contains emoji or astral-plane characters.
drifts.failureScenario: A small model degenerates by looping a paragraph that ends in an emoji or astral CJK char (e.g. 80 astral chars = 80 scalars but 160 UTF-16 units). Swift's scalar window of 160 covers ~160 chars while Kotlin's 160-unit window covers only ~80 chars; the `count >= window*threshold` guard and the overlapping match count therefore differ. iOS flags the loop and aborts the stream; Android (or vice versa) does not — a genuine per-platform behavioral divergence on exactly the degenerate output the guard exists to catch.

Catalog (12 entries, all pinned SHA-256s, ramGB, sizeLabel, quantization, paramsBillions), the RAM->model band function (recommendedModelID: 1.5/3/4/10 GB thresholds), the smallest-fallback, entry lookup, and the entire ModelRouter (marker lists, coding>multilingual>reasoning>general priority, nonLatin 0.3 / 0x24F threshold, code-point walk, preferredFamilies per task, loadable-pool fallback, largest-in-family and largest-overall tie-breaks) are all line-by-line ALIGNED. .max(by:) vs maxByOrNull both keep the first maximal element, so ties (qwen25-coder-7b vs deepseek-r1-7b at 6.5 GB) resolve identically. Swift hard-codes the memory gate's free-RAM to total (freeGB: ram) with no way to pass real free RAM; Kotlin adds a freeRamGb parameter (default = total) that, when a caller supplies actual free RAM, gates against a smaller budget and can return a smaller 'best installable' model than the Swift twin for the identical device. With the default argument the two agree; the drift is latent until an Android call-site passes real free RAM (which Android, unlike iOS, can obtain). Secondary asymmetry: Swift's recommendation()/HardwareTiers table has no Kotlin counterpart, but it feeds no shared model-id decision, so it does not cause per-platform pick drift on its own.

drifts.description: bestInstallableModel signatures diverge and feed the MemoryFitness gate different free-RAM values. Swift takes only forTotalRAMGB and passes freeGB: ram everywhere (assumes free == total, no override possible). Kotlin adds freeRamGb: Double = totalRamGb and passes it to MemoryFitness.check on both the banded-model probe and the fallback filter. When the default is used the two are identical, but Kotlin uniquely allows a caller to gate against actual free RAM, which makes the gate stricter and can select a smaller model than Swift's twin for the same device.
drifts.failureScenario: 16 GB Android device under memory pressure with ~4 GB free: a screen calls bestInstallableModel(totalRamGb=16.0, freeRamGb=4.0). Kotlin's 85% budget gate now evaluates against 4 GB free, blocks the 11 GB qwen3-14b band pick AND most large fallbacks, and returns a small model (e.g. a ~3-4 GB entry). The iOS twin, calling bestInstallableModel(forTotalRAMGB: 16.0), always gates against 16 GB, keeps qwen3-14b (or the largest fitting), and recommends a materially larger model. Same hardware, two different 'RECOMMENDED FOR THIS DEVICE' picks across platforms.

confirm.reason: REFUTED as a confirmed behavioral drift. The reviewer's line reads are factually correct: Swift bestInstallableModel(forTotalRAMGB:) takes only total RAM and passes freeGB: ram to MemoryFitness.check on both probes; Kotlin bestInstallableModel adds freeRamGb: Double = totalRamGb and passes it to check on both probes. But this is an intentional/inert API-surface asymmetry, not a live per-platform divergence: (1) Default-path equivalence is provable — called the way both twins can be called identically (Kotlin bestInstallableModel(totalRamGb)), freeRamGb defaults to totalRamGb, so both check() calls receive (model, totalRamGb, totalRamGb), byte-for-byte identical to Swift. On every input both platforms accept, they return the same model, and the parity vectors pass. (2) The divergence only fires IF an Android-only caller passes a distinct freeRamGb — no such caller exists in the six named twin files. The `= totalRamGb` default signals the intended normal usage is total-as-budget, matching Swift. (3) The asymmetry mirrors a DOCUMENTED, deliberate convention: Swift's route convenience overload passes freeRAMGB: total precisely because — per its own comment — iOS doesn't reliably expose free memory, so total is the budget. So the extra Kotlin parameter is a latent, unexercised capability defaulting to Swift-identical behavior. Not a confirmed behavioral drift that produces two different picks on equivalent inputs.

drift.summary: Compared all four named file pairs line-by-line. DownloadPolicy is fully aligned (identical allows/reason logic and strings). DownloadStore's core table ops (upsert/updateProgress/setState/remove/resumable) and PersistedDownload.fractionComplete are aligned. ModelIntegrity's magic-header check and streaming SHA-256 are behaviorally equivalent (chunk-size differs 1MB vs 64KB but that never changes a digest). Two candidate drifts were raised in the areas the prompt flagged as high-risk, but BOTH were REFUTED by the second skeptic: (1) the composed accept GATE verify(fileURL:expectedSHA256:) exists only on Swift, but the Kotlin download engine + gate composition deliberately live in the app module outside these core twins — an architecture/coverage asymmetry, not a per-platform behavioral split between the reviewed twin files; (2) PersistedDownload.resumeOffset is Kotlin-only but is a dead, unconsumed accessor whose described failure lives in out-of-scope app-layer engines. Net: no drift survived the second skeptic for this subsystem.

drifts.description: The composed integrity ACCEPT GATE verify(fileURL:expectedSHA256:) exists only on Swift. It enforces: magic header ALWAYS (throw notGGUF first), then SHA-256 ONLY when expectedSHA256 is non-nil AND non-empty (the `!expected.isEmpty` short-circuit), with a case-insensitive compare. The Kotlin core ModelIntegrity object provides the primitives (hasGGUFMagic, sha256Hex) but has NO verify() composing them, and no ModelIntegrityException-throwing gate. Whatever calls the gate on Android (app module) is free to compose these differently — e.g. run SHA-256 even for empty/blank expected, or check checksum before/without the magic gate — with no shared, unit-tested definition. The one decision that separates 'downloaded' from 'loaded' is unported in core.
drifts.failureScenario: Catalog pins expectedSHA256 = "" (empty) for a model. On Swift, verify() skips the checksum branch and accepts on magic-header alone. If the Android app-module gate instead treats empty as 'compute and compare', it computes SHA-256(file) and compares against "", never matching, so the model is rejected on Android but accepted on iOS — a per-platform behavior split for the identical catalog entry.

confirm.reason: REFUTED — this is an architectural/coverage observation, not a behavioral drift between the twin files. Both cited locations are read correctly: Swift ModelIntegrity.swift:41-52 does have the composed gate verify(fileURL:expectedSHA256:); Kotlin ModelIntegrity.kt:16-45 does provide only the primitives (hasGGUFMagic, sha256Hex) plus a bare ModelIntegrityException, with no verify() composing them. But a twin DRIFT requires two pieces of executable logic that, on the same input, behave differently. There is no such Kotlin logic to compare against — the Android core deliberately contains NO real download engine and NO gate composition. The named files document why: Swift ships the real engine in core, whereas Kotlin ModelDownloader.kt states the real Android downloader lives in the app module (WorkManager + a resumable foreground download) and core ships only the seam + MockModelDownloader. The gate composition tracks the engine's layer. Every failure scenario is conditional on hypothetical app-module code that is out of scope and not shown to exist. It is a legitimate note that Swift codifies the gate in CI-tested core while Android leaves it uncodified in an untested app layer, but that is a test-coverage/architecture asymmetry, not a per-platform behavioral split between the reviewed twin files.

drifts.description: PersistedDownload.resumeOffset is Kotlin-only. Kotlin computes the HTTP Range resume offset as bytesDownloaded for every state EXCEPT COMPLETED (which yields 0). Swift's PersistedDownload has no resumeOffset property at all — only fractionComplete. There is no shared definition of the resume byte-offset, so the 'resume from N bytes' contract lives on one platform only. Notably Kotlin returns bytesDownloaded even for FAILED records (only COMPLETED zeroes it), which is itself a debatable choice with no Swift counterpart to reconcile against.
drifts.failureScenario: On relaunch, resumable() returns a record in state FAILED with bytesDownloaded = 900MB. Android code reading resumeOffset issues a Range: bytes=900000000- request and resumes from a possibly-corrupt partial (offset survives a FAILED state). iOS has no resumeOffset, so its resume path (if any) must derive the offset independently — the two platforms cannot be guaranteed to send the same Range header for the same persisted record.

confirm.reason: REFUTED as a behavioral drift (false positive). The cited structural asymmetry is factually real but inert. Verified both locations: Kotlin DownloadStore.kt:21-23 does define resumeOffset (get() = if (state != State.COMPLETED) bytesDownloaded else 0L), and Swift PersistedDownload has no such property. However: (1) DEAD GETTER — NO CONSUMER. resumeOffset is a pure computed property that NO method in the Kotlin twin files reads. A property nobody consumes cannot produce divergent runtime behavior; the two resumable() implementations are byte-for-byte equivalent. (2) THE DESCRIBED FAILURE LIVES IN OUT-OF-SCOPE CODE. The failure scenario concerns the actual byte-fetching download engines, which BOTH twins explicitly delegate to the app layer OUTSIDE these files. Neither twin's in-scope downloader resumes at all (Swift's ChunkedDownloadDelegate always deletes the partial and starts from zero; Kotlin's MockModelDownloader ignores resume entirely), so no Range header divergence occurs in the code under review. (3) SEVERITY MISCALIBRATED — at most a completeness/parity gap, currently inert. Verdict: no real behavioral drift in the twin logic; the flagged asymmetry is a dead, unconsumed accessor.

drift.summary: The gate (Capability), single-action execute path, plan pre-flight ordering/numbering, aggregate approval, fail-closed approver, sequential-stop, and ledger truncation bound (200) are all aligned. Two real drifts found, both in the exception-handling of the preview path. (1) In executePlan's all-or-nothing pre-flight, Swift guards capability.plan() with `try?` and returns a graceful "Couldn't preview step N. Nothing was done." refusal on a throwing preview, whereas Kotlin calls plan() completely unguarded, so a preview that throws propagates out of executePlan as an uncaught exception instead of a clean fail-closed refusal. (2) On the single-action path, Swift wraps CapabilityGate.assess in do/catch (plan is `async throws`) while Kotlin's assess/plan are declared non-throwing and are called without a try/catch, so a runtime exception in plan() is neither ledgered as "error" nor converted to the graceful "Couldn't preview" observation — it propagates, breaking the flight-recorder invariant.

drifts.description: In executePlan's all-or-nothing pre-flight, Swift wraps the per-step preview in `guard let preview = try? await item.capability.plan(item.input)` and, on a throwing/failing preview, returns the graceful fail-closed refusal "Couldn't preview step N (name). Nothing was done." Kotlin instead calls `previews.add(item.first.plan(item.second))` with no guard whatsoever. A capability whose plan() throws is handled as a clean, nothing-done refusal on iOS but propagates as an uncaught exception out of executePlan on Android — the exact fail-closed pre-flight guarantee the plan pre-flight is supposed to give (one bad item refuses the whole plan, nothing runs) is lost on the Kotlin side.
drifts.failureScenario: A plan contains a T2+ capability whose plan(input) throws (e.g. it inspects the target path during preview and the resource is missing/permission-denied). On iOS: executePlan returns "Couldn't preview step 2 (name). Nothing was done." and no step runs. On Android: the exception escapes executePlan uncaught, tearing down the agent turn instead of returning a controlled refusal — divergent per-platform behavior on identical input, and a pre-flight that no longer fails closed cleanly.

confirm.reason: CONFIRMED real drift — the first reviewer is correct on both cited lines. Swift declares plan() as throws (Capability.swift:57) and guards EVERY call site: in executePlan via `guard let preview = try? await item.capability.plan(item.input) else { return "Couldn't preview step N ... Nothing was done." }` (CapabilityRunner.swift:174-176), and in the single-execute path via a do/catch around CapabilityGate.assess. Kotlin declares plan() non-throwing (Capability.kt:47) and guards NO call site: executePlan does a bare `previews.add(item.first.plan(item.second))` (CapabilityRunner.kt:103), and the single-execute path calls CapabilityGate.assess with no try/catch. Kotlin has no checked exceptions, but a T2+ capability override of plan() can still throw at runtime. On that input: iOS returns the fail-closed "Couldn't preview step N (name). Nothing was done." and runs nothing; Android lets the exception escape executePlan uncaught, tearing down the agent turn — divergent per-platform behavior on identical input, and the all-or-nothing pre-flight guarantee is lost on Android. This is not inert: the Kotlin author DID guard the run() loop with catch (t: Throwable), proving fail-closed was the intended contract — they simply failed to guard the plan()/assess() preview calls. wrongSide=kotlin is correct; both line citations check out.

drifts.description: On the single-action execute() path, Swift wraps `CapabilityGate.assess(...)` (which awaits the throwing `plan()`) in a do/catch that logs decision "error" and returns "Couldn't preview <name>: <error>". Kotlin calls `CapabilityGate.assess(...)` directly inside the `when` with no try/catch; because Kotlin declares plan()/assess() as non-throwing, the compiler doesn't force handling, so a runtime exception thrown by a preview on this path is neither ledgered as "error" nor converted to the graceful "Couldn't preview" observation — it propagates. Partly a language signature difference (Swift `async throws` vs Kotlin non-throwing), but the intent (preview failure => logged error + graceful observation) is enforced on Swift and unenforced on Kotlin.
drifts.failureScenario: A single mutating capability's plan() throws at runtime (e.g. NPE / IllegalState while building the preview). iOS: ledger gets a "error" row with outcome "preview failed: ..." and the loop receives "Couldn't preview <name>: ...". Android: no "error" ledger row is written for the preview failure and the exception escapes execute(), so the flight-recorder invariant (every invocation, including failed previews, is recorded) is broken on Android for this case.

confirm.reason: CONFIRMED — real behavioral drift; wrong side is Kotlin. Both cited locations are accurate. Swift execute() wraps CapabilityGate.assess() (which internally awaits the throwing plan(), plan declared async throws) in a do/catch (CapabilityRunner.swift:110-116): a throwing preview is ledgered as decision "error" with outcome "preview failed: <error>" and returns "Couldn't preview <name>: <error>". Kotlin execute() calls CapabilityGate.assess() bare in the when head (CapabilityRunner.kt:44) with no try/catch; assess() and plan() carry non-throwing signatures, so the compiler never forces handling. The key skeptical check — can a Kotlin plan() actually throw despite its signature? — resolves against refutation: on the JVM ALL exceptions are unchecked, so a non-throwing signature is not a runtime guarantee. Any real T2+ capability whose plan() builds a preview by inspecting input/filesystem can throw. Such an exception propagates uncaught out of Android's execute(): no "error" ledger row is written and the exception escapes, breaking the flight-recorder invariant that iOS upholds. The asymmetry is systematic, not a one-off (same gap on executePlan). Within each file the asymmetry is also visible: Kotlin DOES wrap capability.run() in try/catch but leaves the assess()/plan() call unguarded, whereas Swift guards both throwing surfaces.

drift.summary: The keyword list, category ordering, isBlocked/matches control flow, multi-word substring path, and match ordering are byte-for-byte equivalent across the twins. One behavioral drift exists in single-word boundary matching: Swift uses NSRegularExpression's ICU `\b`, while Kotlin deliberately swaps to a lookaround over `[\p{L}\p{N}_]` (to avoid an Android regex crash). These two "word char" definitions diverge on Unicode nonspacing combining marks (\p{Mn}, e.g. U+0301). ICU's `\b` counts a combining mark as part of the word, so Swift finds NO boundary and does NOT block; Java's `\p{L}\p{N}_` class excludes \p{M}, so the Kotlin lookahead succeeds and DOES block. Verified empirically: `"pin"+U+0301` gives swift=false, kotlin-pattern=true. Result: a decomposed-Unicode token adjacent to a single-word keyword is withheld on Android but allowed to pass on iOS/macOS. The other classic divergence points (underscore \p{Pc}, non-decimal numerals \p{No}/\p{Nl}, precomposed accents \p{L}) were tested and agree on both engines — no drift there. The Kotlin comment (lines 33-34) explicitly claims iOS-ICU parity for accented text but only covers the PRECOMPOSED case ("piné"); it misses this decomposed combining-mark case, so the comment is subtly wrong.

drifts.description: Single-word boundary matching uses two different 'word character' definitions. Swift's ICU `\b` counts a Unicode nonspacing combining mark (category \p{Mn}, e.g. U+0301 combining acute) as part of the word, so no boundary forms after a keyword followed by a bare combining mark and the keyword does NOT match. Kotlin's lookaround `(?<![\p{L}\p{N}_])KEYWORD(?![\p{L}\p{N}_])` excludes \p{M} from its word class, so the same combining mark is treated as a non-word char, the boundary forms, and the keyword DOES match. Concretely: haystack = "pin" + U+0301 → Swift isBlocked=false, Kotlin isBlocked=true (empirically confirmed against NSRegularExpression and the exact Kotlin pattern). Underscore (\p{Pc}), non-decimal numerals (½ \p{No}, Ⅻ \p{Nl}), and precomposed accents (é \p{L}) were all tested and agree on both engines, so combining marks are the sole divergence.
drifts.failureScenario: An assistant/agent message contains a single-word blocked keyword immediately followed by a decomposed Unicode combining mark, e.g. the byte sequence "pin" + U+0301 (or "pay"/"buy"/"delete"/"password" + any \p{Mn} mark). On Android the message is flagged/blocked (isBlocked returns true); on iOS/macOS the identical message passes the safety check (isBlocked returns false) and the agent is permitted to act on it. Same input, opposite safety decision per platform — a per-platform hole in a hard safety sandbox. The Kotlin source comment claiming full iOS-ICU accented-text parity only holds for precomposed accents, not this decomposed case.

confirm.reason: CONFIRMED — real behavioral drift, empirically verified on this machine by running both code paths on identical inputs. Swift (SafetyBlocklist.swift:40-41) builds `\b<keyword>\b` and matches via NSRegularExpression, which uses ICU's DEFAULT word-boundary mode whose word-char set INCLUDES \p{M} marks. Kotlin (SafetyBlocklist.kt:37 compile + :48 find) uses an explicit lookaround `(?<![\p{L}\p{N}_])<kw>(?![\p{L}\p{N}_])` whose word class EXCLUDES \p{M}. So a combining nonspacing mark after a keyword is a word-char to Swift (boundary suppressed → no match) but a non-word-char to Kotlin (boundary forms → match). Direct side-by-side run, identical inputs: "pin"+U+0301 → Swift isBlocked=FALSE, Kotlin isBlocked=TRUE (DRIFT); "pay"+U+0301 → Swift FALSE, Kotlin TRUE (DRIFT). Control cases all AGREE: plain "pin"→both true; "opinion"/"repin"→both false; "pin_"→both false; "pin½"→both false; precomposed "piné"→both false. Severity/direction nuance for triage: wrongSide is genuinely both-disagree, but the security-relevant hole is on the SWIFT side (iOS/macOS): it FAILS TO BLOCK a keyword+combining-mark, so the hard safety sandbox has a per-platform bypass on iOS/macOS while Android over-blocks (fails safe). The Kotlin comment claiming iOS-ICU parity for accented text only holds for precomposed accents, not this decomposed combining-mark case.

drift.summary: ThermalThrottle, ThermalGovernor, ThreadPlanner.recommend, and the ThermalBattery numeric estimates are line-for-line aligned. The unknown-thermal-state fallback (Swift @unknown default → .serious vs Kotlin else → CRITICAL) was RAISED as a P1 but REFUTED: the two branches map non-isomorphic input domains (Apple's 4-case enum capped at .critical vs Android's 7 status ints), the Swift @unknown default is unreachable dead code today, and the Kotlin else legitimately handles EMERGENCY/SHUTDOWN which Apple has no equivalent for — so no real hardware condition produces divergent throttling. The one CONFIRMED drift is MemoryFitness: Swift computes and reports a concrete remaining-GB figure in the WARNING message (and exposes remainingAfterLoadGB), while Kotlin drops the remaining computation entirely and shows a vague "system tight" message with a struct that lacks the field. The load/block DECISIONS are identical on both sides; only the warning-message content and one struct field drift.

drifts.description: The unknown/unexpected thermal-state fallback disagrees: Swift's `@unknown default` returns `.serious`, but Kotlin's `else` branch returns `ThermalLevel.CRITICAL`. This is not just the EMERGENCY/SHUTDOWN mapping — it is the catch-all for any status value the `when` does not enumerate. The two platforms therefore resolve an unrecognized thermal reading to different levels, which flows into ThermalThrottle: SERIOUS halves the thread count (base/2) while CRITICAL pins it to 1 thread.
drifts.failureScenario: A future/unknown thermal status arrives (e.g. an OS adds a new ProcessInfo.ThermalState case on iOS, or PowerManager returns a value outside 0-6 on Android). On iOS the governor drops to base/2 threads (SERIOUS); on Android the same unknown condition drops to a single thread (CRITICAL). Identical hardware condition, different sustained inference throughput and heat profile per platform.

confirm.reason: REFUTED as a P1 behavioral drift. The two cited lines map different, non-isomorphic input domains: Swift's level(from:) takes ProcessInfo.ThermalState (a 4-case Swift enum capped at .critical), and its @unknown default → .serious (ThermalMonitor.swift:22) is UNREACHABLE dead code with today's SDKs — all four real cases are enumerated, so no thermal reading on any shipping iOS/macOS device ever hits it. Kotlin's levelFromStatus(status: Int) takes a raw Android int 0-6, and its else → CRITICAL (ThermalMonitor.kt:23) is a LIVE, deliberate mapping for status 5/6 (EMERGENCY/SHUTDOWN) — states Apple's API has no equivalent for — plus a catch-all. For every thermal input that actually exists on each platform today, the twins agree monotonically (hotter → fewer threads). The only "disagreement" is on a hypothetical FUTURE Apple ThermalState case that does not exist, so it produces zero divergence on any real hardware condition. The APIs are structurally different (4 vs 7 levels, with Android's top states above Apple's max), so a bit-identical unknown fallback is not even well-defined. This is at most a latent low-value consistency nit on unreachable code, not a P1 per-platform throttling bug.

drifts.description: The WARNING-severity message content drifts. Swift computes `remaining = freeGB - required` (line 37) and tells the user the concrete number: "<label> will leave only <remaining>GB free. System may be slow." Kotlin never computes `remaining` at all and emits a vague, numberless "<label> will leave the system tight." Same memory condition, materially different user-facing guidance; Kotlin also cannot show how much headroom is left because it never derives it.
drifts.failureScenario: A model whose post-load usage lands between the 0.65 warning and 0.85 hard budgets: iOS users see "will leave only 1.2GB free. System may be slow." (actionable), Android users see "will leave the system tight." (no number). The twin surfaces are supposed to be the same honest 'what to expect' string; they are not.

confirm.reason: CONFIRMED — real behavioral drift in the MemoryFitness twin. Both twins enter the WARNING branch on the identical condition `usageAfterLoad > budgetWarning`, so the same model/memory state triggers it on both platforms. Swift (MemoryFitness.swift:37) computes remaining = freeGB - required and its WARNING message (line 57) surfaces the concrete number: "<label> will leave only <remaining>GB free. System may be slow." Kotlin (MemoryFitness.kt:29) computes only usageAfterLoad and never derives remaining; its WARNING message (line 37) is the vague, numberless "<label> will leave the system tight." — dropping both the headroom figure and the "System may be slow." guidance. The BLOCKED and SAFE messages are byte-identical between twins; only the WARNING branch drifts. Corroborating: Kotlin's MemoryCheckResult data class has no remainingAfterLoadGB field at all, so the Kotlin side structurally cannot carry the headroom number. Wrong side is Kotlin — it deviates from the Swift/desktop original. (The only nit is the Kotlin message literal is on line 37 not 36, which does not affect the finding.)

drifts.description: MemoryCheckResult has a drifted shape. Swift exposes six fields including `availableMemoryGB`, `requiredMemoryGB`, and `remainingAfterLoadGB`. Kotlin exposes only `canLoad, severity, requiredGB, availableGB, message` — it omits `remainingAfterLoadGB` entirely and renames the memory fields. Any caller/telemetry consuming the result for the remaining-headroom figure works on iOS and has no equivalent on Android.
drifts.failureScenario: UI or logging code that displays remaining-after-load headroom (the value Swift carries in remainingAfterLoadGB) has a per-platform gap: it can render the figure on iOS but the Android result type has no such field, so the Android UI either omits it or must recompute it out-of-band, risking divergence from the Swift value.

confirm.reason: CONFIRMED (not refuted). Verified both cited files in full. Struct shape: Swift MemoryCheckResult (MemoryFitness.swift:9-16) has 6 fields including availableMemoryGB, requiredMemoryGB, remainingAfterLoadGB. Kotlin MemoryCheckResult (MemoryFitness.kt:5-11) has 5 fields: canLoad, severity, requiredGB, availableGB, message — no remainingAfterLoadGB, and the memory fields are renamed. The behavioral (user-visible) drift the field-shape drives: the remaining-headroom figure is carried only for display, and the two twins DISPLAY it differently (Swift shows the concrete GB in the WARNING message; Kotlin shows a static numberless string). wrongSide=kotlin is correct. Scope note: this is NOT a decision drift — canLoad/severity are computed identically on both sides (same 0.85/0.65 budgets, same 1.15/1.30 overhead, same paramsBillions<=3 split, same usageAfterLoad formula and branch order). So a parity checker on the decision vectors would pass. The drift is confined to telemetry/UI output. The other three twin pairs (ThermalMonitor/Throttle/Governor, ThermalBattery, ThreadPlanner) are ALIGNED.

drift.summary: OfflineReadiness.swift/.kt are behaviorally aligned (same 85% threshold, same guard, same truncating pct). But the OnboardingModel twins diverge substantially in the install/prepare path. Real per-platform drifts, all in the areas flagged (restore-through-verify, network gating, download-policy wiring, cancel recovery): (1) the SHA-256 re-verify-and-delete gate on an existing file exists only in Swift — Android trusts and loads a possibly-unverified leftover; (2) Swift's disk-space preflight is entirely absent on Android, and Android runs the cellular gate unconditionally at the top rather than only when a download is actually needed, so an already-downloaded model can be spuriously blocked on cellular. The relaunch fast-paths themselves are structurally equivalent, but they inherit these install-path drifts because both delegate to install()/acceptAndPrepare().

drifts.description: Swift install() re-runs ModelIntegrity.verify(expectedSHA256:) on any pre-existing file at the destination and DELETES it on failure, then falls through to a fresh download — the 'restore-through-verify' gate. Kotlin acceptAndPrepare() has NO integrity re-verification; it calls downloader.download(model) which (per the restore() comment, line 210) 'returns the existing path without re-fetching', so a leftover file is trusted and loaded as-is. Both sides' relaunch-fast-path comments claim the integrity gate re-runs before trusting the file, but on Android that gate simply does not exist.
drifts.failureScenario: A model file was moved into place but the process was killed in the crash window before the multi-GB SHA-256 check finished (a truncated/corrupt GGUF on disk). On iOS, install() re-verifies, the check fails, the bad file is deleted, and a clean re-download happens. On Android, restoreAtLaunch()/acceptAndPrepare() sees the file, skips straight to engine.load on the corrupt bytes, and either crashes the native loader or loads a corrupted model — the exact failure the C3 integrity gate exists to prevent.

confirm.reason: CONFIRMED — real drift, not a false positive. Both cited locations check out on a line-by-line comparison. SWIFT (has the gate): OnboardingModel.swift:190-194 — inside install(), if a file already exists at destination, it runs ModelIntegrity.verify(fileURL: destination, expectedSHA256: model.sha256); on failure (result == nil) it removeItem(at: destination). It then sees the file gone and falls into a fresh download. This matches its own fast-path comment (start()). KOTLIN (missing the gate): OnboardingModel.kt:148-207 — acceptAndPrepare() contains NO integrity verification of any kind. The full flow is: synchronized isInstalling guard → network preflight → downloader.download(model){...} → engine.load(model, path). There is no ModelIntegrity/SHA-256/verify call in the entire file. Line 210's own comment confirms the leftover is trusted as-is: restore()/download "returns the existing path without re-fetching." And restoreAtLaunch()'s comment falsely claims acceptAndPrepare re-runs the download integrity gate — that gate does not exist in the Kotlin code. WRONG SIDE: Kotlin. FAILURE MODE is concrete: a GGUF moved into place but the process killed before the multi-GB SHA-256 check finished (truncated/corrupt bytes). iOS re-verifies → deletes → clean re-download; Android loads corrupt bytes. Also compared the two OfflineReadiness twins end to end: Status cases, the fileExists && size>0 guard, the >= expected * 0.85 ready threshold, and the downloaded/expected*100 pct formula all match. No drift there.

drifts.description: Swift runs BOTH the disk-space preflight (storageCheck, lines 216-220) and the cellular network gate (lines 226-229) ONLY inside the `if !fileExists` block — i.e. only when a download is actually required. Kotlin (a) has NO disk-space preflight anywhere, and (b) runs the cellular gate unconditionally at the very top of acceptAndPrepare (lines 160-166), before it knows whether the file already exists. So Android gates on cellular even when no download is needed, and never refuses a download that cannot fit on disk.
drifts.failureScenario: Two failures. (1) A user on cellular already has the model fully on disk and taps a Settings model-switch (or triggers restoreAtLaunch) to that model: iOS loads it (no network needed, gate skipped); Android hits the unconditional cellular gate and returns Failed('connect to Wi-Fi') despite needing no network.
